title: Releases | Beehive.govt.nz
content:
## Defence Minister to visit Singapore for global security conference
Defence Minister Chris Penk will travel to Singapore this week to attend the Shangri-La Dialogue, an international security conference focused on issues confronting the Indo-Pacific region.

## Remuneration Authority appointments
Workplace Relations and Safety Minister Brooke van Velden has today announced the elevation of Dallas Welch to Chair of the Remuneration Authority [the Authority].

## Government listens to feedback on homeschool regulations
The Government has listened to feedback from the homeschool sector and has agreed to pause work on regulation changes, says Education Minster Erica Stanford.

## Hunting opportunities increase in Hawke’s Bay
Opportunities to hunt in New Zealand are growing, with a new commercial game preserve to open in Hawke’s Bay, increasing outdoor access for Kiwis and boosting the local economy.

## AI Guidance issued to clipboard wielders; how to raise productivity
This week the Government issued guidance to regulators on how to utilise Artificial Intelligence (AI) to raise productivity, Regulation Minister David Seymour says.

## New technology means safer communities
New investment in Budget 2026 will ensure faster, better emergency responses, and make New Zealand more resilient, Emergency Management and Recovery Minister Mark Mitchell says.

## Budget 2026 delivers $100 million investment in Rotorua courts
Courts Minister Nicole McKee has announced a major investment in Rotorua’s justice infrastructure, with Budget 2026 allocating $100 million for new court facilities.

## AI breast screening tool one step closer
The next steps are underway to introduce artificial intelligence (AI) into breast screening, supporting earlier detection and better outcomes for women, Health Minister Simeon Brown says.

## New investigations into heritage and housing rules
An investigation has been launched into whether council planning rules in Wellington and Hamilton are getting in the way of new homes and economic growth.

## Keeping Kiwi music in Kiwi hands
The Government is moving to protect some of New Zealand’s most iconic songs for longer, with changes to the Copyright Act that will back Kiwi artists and bring New Zealand into line with international standards.
